rationale: The cited paper is discussed with a specific finding (early share-distribution feedback in growth stages) and is used to support the citing paper's theoretical expectation that persistent effects are strongest early and decline over time. It is not just listed in background; it appears in hypothesis development, empirical expectation setting, and conclusion where results are interpreted as confirming that prior pattern. The citing paper also uses Bronnenberg et al. (2000) as a methodological comparison point (moving-window analysis) when positioning its own time-varying parameter approach.
relation_description: Osinga et al. build on Bronnenberg et al.'s evidence that persistence-related dynamics are strongest in early market development to motivate and interpret their own launch-to-maturity persistence analysis. They treat Bronnenberg et al. as prior empirical grounding while extending the question to brand-level pharmaceutical marketing effects with a different modeling framework.
